incident_status_summary.incident_commanders_agency_or_organizati: IC: Battalion Chief Laura M. Hernandez, Cal Fire Amador-El Dorado Unit (AEU)
incident_status_summary.incident_management_organization: Type 3 Incident Management Team – Cal Fire AEU / El Dorado County OES Unified Command
incident_status_summary.current_incident_size_or_area_involved_u: 1,420 acres (mixed conifer forest and wildland-urban interface)
incident_status_summary.incident_definition: The Cedar Ridge Wildfire incident encompasses all fire activity, suppression operations, evacuations, and associated impacts resulting from a wind-driven wildland fire that originated near Mile Marker 12 on Cedar Ridge Road in eastern El Dorado County, CA, and has since spread into adjacent private timberlands and residential areas within the Cedar Ridge Estates and North Fork communities. The incident includes structure protection operations, evacuation and repopulation activities, road closures, utility disruptions, and all coordinated emergency response actions under unified command.
incident_status_summary.incident_complexity_level: Type 3 expanding incident, potential to transition to Type 2 if weather and fire behavior worsen

approval__routing_information.primary_location_organization_or_agency_: El Dorado County Office of Emergency Services, 330 Fair Lane, Placerville, CA 95667

incident_location_information.unit_or_other: Cal Fire Amador-El Dorado Unit (AEU), Cedar Ridge Initial Attack Zone
incident_location_information.incident_jurisdiction: Primary: Cal Fire Amador-El Dorado Unit; Local: El Dorado County Sheriff’s Office and El Dorado County Fire Protection District
incident_location_information.incident_location_ownership_if_different: Mixed ownership: Sierra Pacific Industries (private timber), El Dorado County, and multiple private residential landowners in Cedar Ridge Estates and North Fork communities
incident_location_information.legal_description_township_section_range: T11N R12E, Sections 17, 18, 19, 20, Mount Diablo Meridian (approximate)
incident_location_information.short_location_or_area_description_list_: Fire origin is approximately 4.5 miles northeast of downtown Placerville, CA, near Mile Marker 12 on Cedar Ridge Road, just north of the North Fork of the Cosumnes River. Affected areas include: Cedar Ridge Estates subdivision (west and south flanks), scattered rural residences along Cedar Ridge Road and North Fork Road, portions of privately owned industrial timberland managed by Sierra Pacific Industries, and adjacent BLM parcels. The fire is burning in steep, dissected terrain with elevations ranging from 2,400 to 3,200 feet, in mixed conifer, oak woodland, and cured grass understory within a wildland-urban interface zone.
incident_location_information.note_any_electronic_geospatial_data_incl: Incident perimeter and hotspot data collected via infrared (IR) flight on 2025-12-10 at 23:45 PST; data provided as ESRI shapefiles and GeoJSON layers labeled: 'CedarRidge_Perim_20251210_2345', 'CedarRidge_Hotspots_20251210_2345'. Additional operational maps generated in ArcGIS Pro with layers for structures-at-risk, evacuation zones, and dozer lines; PDFs attached to ICS 204 and posted to the incident SharePoint site for operational period 3.

incident_summary.significant_events_for_the_time_period_r:
During the 24-hour operational period from 2025-12-10 06:00 to 2025-12-11 06:00, the Cedar Ridge Wildfire exhibited moderate to active fire behavior, with periods of rapid upslope runs in the afternoon aligned with terrain and gusty southwest winds. Morning relative humidity recoveries were fair, which allowed crews to make meaningful progress on line construction before conditions deteriorated in the afternoon.

Overnight (night shift beginning 12-10 at 18:00), crews focused on holding and improving previously constructed dozer and hand line along the western flank above Cedar Ridge Estates. Two spot fires (0.25 and 0.5 acres) crossed the line around 22:15 due to rolling material on steep slopes; these were quickly detected by an engine patrol and contained within 45 minutes with support from a Type 2 hand crew and a Type 6 engine. No additional slop-overs were reported during the night.

On the southern flank, structure protection operations continued in the Cedar Ridge Estates subdivision and along North Fork Road. Engines conducted triage on approximately 65 additional residences, focusing on clearing flammable materials from around structures, connecting garden hoses to ensure defensible space coverage, and pre-positioning sprinkler kits on four homes with heavy ornamental vegetation. One unoccupied outbuilding (a small wooden shed) was destroyed by radiant heat at approximately 19:40; no primary residences were lost during this period.

Day shift on 12-10 saw increased fire activity beginning around 13:30 as temperatures rose into the mid-70s (unseasonably warm for December) and winds increased to 12–18 mph with gusts up to 25 mph from the southwest. The fire made a short uphill run on the northeast flank toward a ridge line above North Fork Road, adding approximately 120 acres to the total incident size. Air resources (two Type 1 helicopters and one fixed-wing air tanker) conducted multiple water and retardant drops to check the head of the fire and support ground crews constructing indirect line along the ridge.

Evacuation orders remained in effect for the entire Cedar Ridge Estates subdivision and for properties along North Fork Road from Mile Marker 9 to Mile Marker 14. The El Dorado County Sheriff’s Office reported that compliance with evacuation orders is high, with an estimated 95% of residents out of the area. Deputies had several in-person contacts with residents attempting to re-enter to check on animals and property; these individuals were escorted in briefly during lulls in fire activity when it was safe to do so. An evacuation warning remains in place for the Outingdale community to the east, although the fire did not significantly advance toward that area during this period.

The Red Cross shelter at El Dorado High School in Placerville continued operations, housing 47 evacuees overnight (down from 62 the previous night) as some residents opted to stay with family or friends. Shelter staff report that several elderly evacuees expressed anxiety about prolonged displacement and concern about prescription refills; the County Health and Human Services Agency deployed a small team to assist with medication coordination and mental health support. Animal Services reports 23 dogs, 11 cats, and 3 goats being housed at the temporary large-animal shelter at the El Dorado County Fairgrounds.

On the infrastructure side, Pacific Gas and Electric (PG&E) de-energized a local distribution line that runs through the fire area to reduce ignition risk and protect damaged infrastructure. This resulted in a planned power outage affecting approximately 410 customers in and around Cedar Ridge Estates. PG&E crews staged near the incident but were not yet granted access to the interior of the burn area due to active fire behavior and snags along access roads. One wooden utility pole was confirmed destroyed; additional damage assessments will be conducted once access is safe.

A unified command structure between Cal Fire AEU, El Dorado County Sheriff’s Office, and El Dorado County Fire Protection District remained in place. A community briefing was conducted virtually via the county’s Facebook Live platform at 19:00, with approximately 350 live viewers and over 2,000 views recorded by the end of the operational period. Incident leadership provided updates on fire progress, evacuation status, air quality concerns, and expectations for the next 24–48 hours. Questions from the public focused heavily on when residents might be able to return home, how to obtain re-entry passes, and what documentation would be needed for insurance claims.

Resources on the incident during this period included: 10 Type 3 engines, 6 Type 1 municipal engines, 4 Type 6 engines, 4 Type 2 hand crews, 2 dozers, 3 water tenders, 2 Type 1 helicopters, 1 Type 2 helicopter, and 1 fixed-wing air tanker available on an as-needed basis. Total personnel assigned during this period was approximately 215. No firefighter injuries were reported, though there were two near-miss events involving rolling rocks and a snag that fell unexpectedly near a saw team; these have been documented, and a safety stand-up was conducted at the start of the next operational period to reinforce downhill line construction and snag hazard protocols.

Air quality in the immediate area remained in the ‘Unhealthy for Sensitive Groups’ range, with heavy smoke pooling in low-lying drainages overnight and early morning. The county issued an air quality advisory, encouraging residents, particularly those with respiratory conditions, to remain indoors with windows closed and to utilize HEPA filters if available.

Overall, while the fire grew moderately in size, key objectives were met during this time period: no primary residences were lost, no serious injuries occurred, evacuation perimeters held, and containment increased from 25% to 35% as crews continued to secure line on the western and southern flanks. The incident remains dynamic, with continued concern about forecasted winds over the next 24–48 hours and the potential for spotting across North Fork Road into heavier timber fuels.
incident_summary.primary_materials_or_hazards_involved_ha:
Primary hazards are associated with a wildland-urban interface wildfire burning in mixed fuels. Fuels include: (1) Timber litter and understory – mixed conifer (ponderosa pine, Douglas fir, incense cedar) with heavy accumulation of dead and down material and a thick duff layer; (2) Brush – manzanita, ceanothus, and dense chaparral patches on south-facing slopes; (3) Grass – cured annual and perennial grasses, particularly along roadways and in previously mowed fields.

Secondary hazards include: (a) Hazardous household materials in threatened and impacted structures, such as propane tanks (250–500 gallon above-ground tanks observed at numerous residences), gasoline stored in garages and sheds, pesticides, pool chemicals, and common cleaning agents; (b) Utility hazards from damaged or compromised power poles and lines (PG&E infrastructure), including potential for downed energized lines and falling poles; (c) Hazardous trees (snags) created by fire damage in steep terrain, posing significant risks to firefighters along line construction and access routes.

There are no known releases of industrial hazardous chemicals, infectious agents, or radiological materials associated with this incident at this time. However, responders are advised to use standard structural firefighting PPE when operating in and around structures due to the potential for exposure to combustion by-products, asbestos in older structures, and other toxic smoke components. All personnel are reminded to use respiratory protection as appropriate when working in heavy smoke conditions, and to monitor for heat-related illness due to unseasonably warm temperatures for December.
